sections.content:

It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Level 2 has a vertical divider, dark gray L-shape (left panel), yellow L-shape (left panel, below), black L-shape with white dots (right panel) - SPACE makes the yellow shape grow from 61 to 72 pixels (matching dark gray shape's size) and changes dot color from sky blue to white - After SPACE, DOWN now moves the dark gray shape by 3 rows - LEFT/RIGHT moves the dark gray shape by 6 cols, but the direction seems inconsistent - need to re-verify - Current: dark gray at rows 42-53, cols 21-35; yellow at rows 42-53, cols 3-17 - Goal: align dark gray shape with yellow shape (same position)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 75 actions; a strong score needs about 50 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! UP moved the dark gray shape from rows 36-47 to rows 33-44, but RIGHT didn't move it (still at cols 0-14). So RIGHT doesn't work at rows 33-44, cols 0-14.
Looking at the image, the dark gray shape is now at rows 33-44, cols 0-14. The yellow shape is at rows 42-53, cols 3-17. The overlap is at rows 42-44, cols 3-14. But the image shows the dark gray shape is ABOVE the yellow shape, with no overlap. This is because the dark gray L-shape doesn't extend to rows 42-44 at cols 3-14.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, the dark gray shape and yellow shape are now adjacent but not overlapping. The dark gray shape is at rows 33-44, and the yellow shape is at rows 42-53. They share rows 42-44, but the dark gray shape might not have pixels at those rows in the overlapping columns.
I've been exploring this level for many actions without finding the solution. Let me try a completely different approach. Maybe the goal is not about overlapping shapes but about matching the black shape's pattern.
Looking at the black shape on the right, it has 8 white dots inside it. The dots are arranged in a specific pattern. Maybe I need to create the same pattern with the dark gray shape by overlapping it with the yellow shape.
Let me try to systematically find the position where the dark gray shape creates 8 yellow dots (matching the black shape's 8 white dots).
Actually, I just noticed something. The RIGHT action didn't work at rows 33-44, cols 0-14. This suggests that RIGHT might be blocked by something at that position. Let me check if there's a wall or boundary.
Actually, looking at the segmentation, the dark gray shape is at cols 0-14, which is at the left edge of the board. RIGHT should move it to cols 6-20, but it didn't. This suggests RIGHT is blocked at this position.
Let me try DOWN from rows 33-44 to see if it works.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Dark gray at rows 33-44, cols 0-14
# RIGHT didn't work, try DOWN
action(['DOWN'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Dark gray: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check for yellow dots
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'Y' and 1 < n['pixels'] < 50:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Yellow small: row {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, col {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Dark gray: row 36-47, col 0-14
Yellow small: row 57-63, col 63-63, pixels=7
Level completed: False
